Computer-vision field study · point-in-time · 17 Jul 2026

How full is the lot?

Four parking cameras, one afternoon, and six ways of answering the same question: two computer-vision pipelines we built, and four vision models asked cold — Claude Haiku & Sonnet, plus Gemma 4 and Qwen 3.5 over Ollama Cloud. Then a twist: show each model the CV pipeline's own detections and let it decide for itself. The methods rarely agree, and the reasons they disagree are the whole point.

Three definitions hiding in one phrase

Raw count ÷ capacity

Detect and count vehicles, divide by how many the lot holds. Objective — if you can count every car and you know the capacity. Both are hard: distant cars vanish, and capacity is usually a guess.

Surface coverage

What fraction of the lot's ground is under metal, corrected for perspective. Needs no capacity number. But a lot with a big empty apron reads “low” even when its rows are packed.

Holistic judgment

Just look and say — what the vision models, and people, actually do. Fast and robust, but unverifiable and prone to reading high.

Raw vehicle count, every camera × method

CameraCV singleCV tiledHaikuSonnetGemma 4Qwen 3.5
Togwotee Mountain Lodge1287899
Security-cam demo lot343031303135
Brighton Ski Resort6267871306272
Solitude — Moonbeam lot1741758082135

Cameras ordered emptiest → fullest. The two CV columns are the same detector, single-pass vs. tiled. On empty or clearly-spaced lots every method lands together; as cars pack and recede the CV count falls behind and the vision models pull ahead — they're most valuable exactly where the detector is weakest. Qwen 3.5 tends to count highest.

The hybrid twist — does showing the model the detections help?

Every model was also run a second way: given the frame with the detector's red vehicle masks and green footprint dots painted on (no lot box, no numbers), and asked to decide for itself. The result is consistent — the markers anchor the model's count toward the detector's. On the packed Moonbeam lot, where the detector badly undercounts, that dragged every model down, away from the truth (Sonnet 80→42, Qwen 135→105). On the clean Brighton lot it reined in an over-count (Sonnet 130→98). CV-assist is only as good as the CV — but the better models used the markers critically, discarding false positives rather than trusting them.

The resort mentioned ~1,200 cars / full for the race / cam sees ~½–⅔ — but that figure doesn't obviously fit this packed frame, so capacity here is treated as unknown.

ReadThe hard case. Single-pass finds 17 of ~80 vehicles, tiling 41. Raw, the models range 75–135 — but hand them the detector's markers and every one collapses toward it: Sonnet 80→42, Qwen 135→105, Gemma 82→52. CV-assist backfires precisely where the detector is blind.

ReadCV does well here — single-pass alone segments 62 cars. Raw VLMs run higher (Sonnet counted 130 after zooming into crops). The markers pull the VLMs back toward the detector (Sonnet 130→98) — here arguably more honest, since 130 was likely high. Coverage (~45%) matches the eye.

ReadThe easy mid-range case. Everyone lands near ~30 vehicles and the markers barely move the count. Fullness splits, though — Haiku ~75%, Sonnet/Gemma ~50% — bracketing the coverage read (44%).

ReadUniversal agreement: ~8–12 vehicles, a quarter or less. Notably, Sonnet used the markers critically here — merging a double-dotted SUV and rejecting a false-positive blob rather than trusting them blindly.

How each method works, in a line

  • CV single-pass — one YOLOv8-nano segmentation pass over the whole frame; count footprints in a hand-drawn lot region, measure silhouette coverage.
  • CV tiled (SAHI) — slice the frame into overlapping crops so a 15-pixel far car becomes a 60-pixel near one, detect each, merge with non-max suppression. A targeted fix for tiny-object recall.
  • Perspective correction — a homography flattens the lot to top-down; its Jacobian (1/w³) weights each pixel by the real ground it covers, so far pixels count as much as near ones.
  • Vision models — raw — hand the model the plain JPEG; ask for a count and a fullness bucket. No region, no detector, no tuning.
  • Vision models — hybrid — same, but the image carries the CV detections (red masks + green dots); the model decides whether to trust them.

Honest limitations

  • Lot regions are first-guess quads, eyeballed, not tuned per camera.
  • Capacities are guesses; the resort's ~1,200-car figure hasn't been pinned to a specific lot yet.
  • Model numbers are single unverified reads — no ground-truth count exists.
  • Each panel is one frame at one instant — not an average, not a trend.

Point-in-time study · frames 17 Jul 2026 · detector YOLOv8-nano (seg) · vision models Claude Haiku 4.5 & Sonnet 5, Gemma 4 & Qwen 3.5 (Ollama Cloud) · all images embedded, nothing phones home.
